climate.note: Hilton Head Island often behaves like 8b due to maritime influence
climate.wind: Occasional strong coastal winds, especially storm season

activities: Begin weekly Liquid Fence on vegetables
Water vegetables every 1-2 days in peak heat
Prune tomato suckers aggressively for airflow
Watch for early blight — remove affected leaves
BEST planting window for shrubs and trees
Don't prune azaleas — buds set in fall

soil.type: Sandy loam, often with clay sublayer
soil.ph: Naturally slightly acidic (5.5-6.5) — ideal for azaleas, gardenias, blueberries
soil.drainage: Generally good due to sand content but varies
soil.saltIntrusion: Near marsh/ocean, soil salinity affects plant selection
soil.bestAmendment: Mushroom compost, aged compost, pine bark fines
warnings: Concrete/stucco debris raises pH dramatically — remove before planting
Don't over-amend veggie beds — too rich causes leafy growth at expense of fruit
Always pH test before planting, especially near construction debris

mulch.best: Natural pine bark mulch or pine straw (acidic, improves soil)
mulch.avoid: Dyed black mulch — absorbs excessive heat, doesn't improve soil
mulch.warning: Weed barrier tarps suffocate root systems — avoid in ornamental beds

deer.severity: Extremely bold on Hilton Head — accustomed to humans
deer.behavior: Travel forest edges constantly, most active dawn/dusk but feed any time
protectionMethods: Liquid Fence spray — reapply after every rain, most effective when consistent
Fishing line barrier at 2-3 ft height — deer dislike invisible barrier
Wire caging — chicken wire cylinder around young trees (4 ft minimum)
Physical fencing — 7-8 ft required to reliably stop deer
Pool/structure proximity — deer less bold near houses

plantingCalendar.notes: Buy transplants, plant deep, bury stem to top 4-5 leaves
Need warm soil, slow to establish, more deer resistant
Train vertically on trellis, high deer risk
After last frost, replaces bolted lettuce
Short window, bolts by June heat
Fall planting ONLY for spring harvest

techniques.tomatoes: Space 24-36 inches for airflow. Plant DEEP — strip lower leaves, bury stem. Prune suckers aggressively. Use cages or stakes.
techniques.peppers: Space 18 inches. Plant deep like tomatoes. Slow to establish — normal to look sad 2-3 weeks.
techniques.cucumbers: Train vertically on trellis. Against south-facing walls for heat. Use T-posts for freestanding trellis.
techniques.lettuce: Space 6-8 inches. Plant shallow. Harvest outer leaves progressively. Stagger plantings 2 weeks apart.

companionPlanting.general: French marigolds as border around entire veggie bed — deters deer, aphids, nematodes. Mint CONTAINER ONLY — will take over a bed in one season.

fungalDisease.pressure: HIGH — humidity creates constant fungal pressure
prevention: Space plants for airflow — never crowd
Water at base, not overhead (soaker hoses)
fungalDisease.treatment: Remove affected leaves immediately, apply copper fungicide

southernIndica.spacing: 5-6 ft apart informal, stagger for natural effect
southernIndica.warning: Avoid Encore azaleas — spread bloom energy, never get full explosive show

gumpo.notes: Nearly bulletproof in Zone 8a. Excellent groundcover under larger azaleas.
gumpo.propagation: Stem cuttings in rooting hormone, roots in 6-8 weeks

kurume.notes: Smaller flowers but completely covered in blooms. Hardier than Southern Indicas.

gardenia.care: Feed with Holly-Tone + chelated iron. Loves acidic soil.
gardenia.freezeNote: Dies back but often recovers from roots. Yellow leaves = freeze stress or iron deficiency.

confederateJasmine.bloom: Intensely fragrant small white star flowers in spring
confederateJasmine.traits: Deer resistant, salt tolerant, nearly indestructible
confederateJasmine.uses: Trellises, fences, groundcover, climbing pool walls

waxMyrtle.uses: Privacy screens, hedges, road frontage screening

screening.spacing: Informal: 6-8 ft. Formal hedge: 4-5 ft. Staggered double row: front 6 ft, back offset 3 ft deeper.

blueberries.care: Love acidic soil, pine needle mulch

brownTurkeyFig.deer: Vulnerable when young — protect with wire cage 2-3 years
brownTurkeyFig.warning: Don't plant over pool/patio — drops fruit

citrus.viability: Marginal in 8a — survives but freeze damage likely
citrus.recovery: Comes back from roots even when top-killed
citrus.care: Protect with frost cloth below 28F. Plant sheltered south-facing near structures.
citrus.diagnosis: Yellow leaves after freeze = stress, not death. Scratch bark — green = alive.

troubleshooting.solution: Remove affected leaves, copper fungicide, space plants wider, soaker hose
Divide late summer, replant 12-18 inch spacing
Scratch bark — green = alive. Wait until late April. Pink roots = life. Cut dead top, water well, be patient.
Remove debris, add sulfur powder or peat moss, pine needle mulch

troubleshooting.cause: Landscape fabric + black dyed mulch killing established plants
troubleshooting.solution: Remove tarp completely. Remove construction debris. Check for surviving roots (pink/red = alive). Water deeply. Wait 3-4 weeks before replanting. pH test.

nurseries: Green Thumb Nursery — local expertise, understands HH conditions
localResources.extension: Clemson Extension — soil testing service, inexpensive, highly recommended
localResources.hoaNotes: Hilton Head plantations have CC&Rs governing landscaping. Check restrictions before planting tall hedges near property lines.

layering.ground: Gumpo azaleas, liriope, Confederate jasmine, ferns

designPrinciples.forestEdge: Deer travel forest edges — plant deer-resistant species here. Azalea walls work beautifully against pine forest. Stagger for natural look.
designPrinciples.poolPatio: Plant fragrant plants near seating (gardenia, Confederate jasmine). No fruit trees over pool. South-facing walls = heat sink for vegetables.
designPrinciples.cottageGarden: Mix perennials, shrubs, herbs, ornamentals. Allow naturalization. Less structured — works with natural landscape.
